sentences.text: Rosa Schachter.
Rosa Siedlecka.
No.
S-I-E-D-L-E-C-K-A.
S-C-H-A-C-H-T-E-R.
I was born in Minsk, Belarus, 15th of December, 1921.
Yes, I had two brothers, two sisters.
I was the third.
Yes.
We a middle-class family.
We moved from Minsk to Konskie when I was a small baby.
K-O-N-S-K-I-E.
Poland.
We lived all those years there.
My parents were religious in a sense that we had two sets of dishes in the house.
Then I had my older brother, who survived the war and died three years ago in London.
My younger--
Stach.
S-T-A-C-H.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The second brother after him, Meyer, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was killed in a raid in the-- in a town called Mooch-- M-I-Z-O-C-H-- which was on the border between Poland and Russia.
He was married.
He had children.
All the family vanished.
Then next I'm in line.
I'm still here, giving this interview [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- oh, no, there was-- I have another sister in Manhattan, older than me.
Forgot.
And her name?
Her name is Nellie Ropper, R-O-P-P-E-R.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She just lost her husband in September.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She has a son in Boston, which is a neurologist.
She herself is a very independent lady.
She's a periodontist.
She teaches.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's a short background.
And then I had a younger sister, Paula.
Beautiful, blond, blue eyes, girl, which was younger, maybe five years, six years than me.
And that was my family.
Father's name was Abraham.
He worked in the lumber business.
He was selling the lumber to certain companies, private.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had good times and bad times.
No, not that I remember.
I was a baby.
I don't know.
But I remember from Poland.
My mother was a typical housewife, devoted to her children.
And pushing all of her children for education, which wasn't easy in Poland.
Hannah.
No.
But when I left her, she was probably around 55 or so.
And my father, too.
I don't know the, the dates.
I'm very bad at dates.
Not on a regular-- but High Holidays.
And it was a middle-class house, household.
Every one of us had friends.
The town itself was a small town of maybe 40,000 people, from-- and 10,000 Jews, about.
In a synagogue nearby.
No.
No, I don't remember names.
Oh, yes, but problems?
Yes.
Because when I was in high school, they-- the Poles actually had numerous clauses at that time.
When I was in high school, a class of--
It's-- they didn't let many Jews go in to school, to high school or colleges.
They-- numerous clauses, which is they discriminate against the Jews.
They didn't let them into schools.
So in a class of, let's say, 35 students, they had only three Jews.
Even then.
Even then.
But everybody knew that anti-Semitism is around the corner because evenings, there were certain streets in town that nobody dared to go.
Because we were afraid they would, they will beat us up.
Very much so.
Very much so.
There was no secret.
Most of them, but in gymnasium, I had mixed colleagues.
I mean, there were Jews, and Jews were only three, but--
Yeah.
One of my girlfriends was-- just died not long ago in Paris.
And one is still someplace in Holland.
He's a dentist.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
And we-- outside the school, we were only between Jews really.
Yeah.
No, they lived in different parts of the city.
And each one came on their own.
No.
No.
As a matter of fact, the, the Poles always underlined-- when my mother used to go to, to parents' meetings, they always underlined that at home, everybody has to speak Polish only in order to--
Yeah, and to the parents.
They used to tell.
Yeah, in order to be perfect in the Polish language.
I was.
Not anymore.
I don't use the language anymore.
I'm glad to speak English
That was a general feeling.
Everybody knew.
I mean, I didn't have to come-- have any special encounters with anybody because everybody was aware of it, that the Poles were anti-Semitic.
You always have to be careful what you say.
And you always could be insulted, but you couldn't answer.
Oh, of course.
That was common knowledge.
Well, at that time, no.
I'm talking about, right now, about high school, and when I was home.
Those things came later.
I was--
Oh, sure.
I remember very well.
Because when I was in high school, my older brother had a job in a nearby town, small town.
Actually, my older brother used to attend the university in Warsaw.
And while he was home, he was-- had a job in a small town near Konskie called Przedborz.
Oh, boy.
That's P-R-Z-E-D-B-O-R-Z.
Przedborz.
He used to keep the books for the Jewish committee there in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in his time off.
And that particular summer, in '39, I was going to him to, spend my vacation.
Because I was off from school.
And that my-- that time, my brother was already married.
And his married family was on the Polish-Russian border, in Ostrog.
O-S-T-R-O-G.
So when he came home, he worked in, in this little town.
So I decided to go that particular summer to visit him and to stay with him because I was free.
That was already in '39.
So you were 18 years old?
Yes, 17, 18 years old.
So I went there, and I used to go before there, too, on my free time from vacation.
Maybe an hour by bus or so.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I reached there, and there already, in '39, was talk about the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At that time, I think they occupied already Czechoslovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a general atmosphere of a war approaching.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was in Przedborz with him, I had a friend, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Meyer, which was so-called my first boyfriend.
He came from Israel.
He-- his parents-- he was the only son.
And his parents send him to Israel to the school of agronomy.
That particular summer, he came home to visit his parents.
So when I was in Przedborz, he called me to come to see him.
There was one problem, though, because Przedborz had a river, which divided the town in two parts.
And there was a bridge.
So I decided to go to visit him.
And I passed the bridge.
And at that time, the atmosphere was really pretty tense because I remember the radios, loudspeakers, the Polish enthusiastic that we never will give in.
We will always win.
And the Polish national songs were played loud.
Actually, we knew that war is coming, but nobody knew when.
So when I passed to visit this friend of-- boyfriend of mine, stayed there a little while.
And in no time, we heard shooting.
The shooting came from the bridge.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he, coming from Israel, already, he had some training.
I don't know.
He told us all, lie down.
So we all lie down on the floor, waiting.
And the shelling and shooting was going on.
So actually, I was separated from my brother.
I couldn't go back over the bridge anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a while, when the shooting stopped, we looked up, and we saw the first Germans coming in.
I was right there when they came.
It was a big panic.
When the shooting stopped, we decided all that-- there were a lot of people in this house because his parents had a bar, where the local people used to come and drink.
And they were all caught by surprise.
So when we got up, and it was quiet, we all ran to the nearest forest.
That was-- because those places were surrounded by forest.
So we run to the forest, and then shooting started again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were running.
Shooting was going on.
I lost my brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after-- What do you mean, you lost your brother?
He was on one side.
I was on the other side.
I never saw him then.
I didn't know where he is.
He didn't know where I am.
And in the forest, we were a couple of hours.
The bombs were coming down.
And shooting's terrible.
Somehow after a while, I met my brother in the forest.
Looks like everybody was running there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I met him, he decided-- there was one fellow with a big truck.
So he decided to load up this truck with people and head toward the east.
Because where he left his family, on the Russian-Polish border, the Russians were there.
That was the part which was taken by the Russian.
I think they made a deal, Stalin and, and Hitler, that this part belongs to Russia, where he left his parents.
They lived there.
So he organized this truck full of people to go, to make their way.
Nobody knew how.
But made their way toward the east, towards the Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was a terrible experience because everybody wanted to get into this truck.
It was so packed, terrible.
A needle couldn't have a place there.
Finally, he had gasoline enough to go a little while.
We went.
At one point, my brother got out from the truck.
He said he has to take a break and breathe some fresh air because it was so sticky.
And he went.
In the meantime, shootings was from all sides.
He got lost.
We were screaming after him, calling.
We couldn't find him anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this truck, after a while, lost gasoline, couldn't-- didn't have any more, couldn't go any further.
So we decided to come back to Przedborz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, the Germans already settled in.
And in the beginning, we still could travel a little.
There was, there was, those were the first days.
So I was eager to go back to Konskie, to my parents.
Did you ever see your brother again?
Later on.
I was eager to see my parents in Konskie.
So when I made my way--
Everything was-- made my way.
By bus.
At that time, we didn't still have any yellow stars.
There were-- the Germans weren't organized, just for the first two, three days, when they came in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went back to my parents.
They were in the house.
We stayed.
But then it started really bad.
They gave the order to put yellow stars, one in front, one in the back.
And we had to wear it all the time.
Then people were going out for some-- you know, right away there was a shortage of food.
And people were going down on the streets to look for something.
They-- I remember there was a square in the middle of the town.
They were just shooting at random.
Yes.
No, we had to make them ourselves.
One in the front on the left side.
In the back.
Yeah, and they always on the left side, parallel with that one.
Yes, every garment has to have one.
You couldn't go without.
Yeah.
Buy all, whatever it may be.
You know, we had in house a piece of yellow material or something.
Everyone had to sew it on.
Yeah.
No food.
And it was dangerous to go out from the house because they were shooting at random.
There were a couple of people killed right in the square of the town.
No, nobody, nobody.
Then there was this rumors that the Germans are going to take the young people.
I mean, nobody really expected what happened later.
Nobody could imagine.
So everybody was in fear of the young people.
So I got in touch-- my mother and father, they insisted that I run.
They were afraid the Germans might take me.
So they insisted I run towards the east.
Why you, not the other siblings?
The other-- the other one, my older brother was-- I lost him in the forest.
The other one lived there, also on the east of Polish-Russian border.
He was married there.
My younger sister was too young.
And my older sister was also married in the east.
And my, and my, and my youngest sister, yeah.
They were all-- my other brother was married there.
And my sister was married there, too, and my other brother was married there.
They wanted me to run towards them, to the east.
So I got in touch with my friend in Przedborz.
He really looked at-- he had the look of a real Polack.
He was tall, blond, blue eyes.
And we decided to just, to take a step.
We two should run towards my other part of the family, there on the east.
We took off the stars.
We went by train, towards the east.
I don't remember which station so which-- how far--
No, not at that time, no papers at all.
We just went on the sheer luck that nobody will recognize us as Jews.
And first we traveled by train towards the border.
We were lucky.
But at that time, wasn't so easy because the trains, you didn't take one train.
You had to take one train.
And then get off, and wait a long time to take another train.
So in between, there were just Poles snooping around to smell out if there's any Jew on the station which was hiding or running.
And they just passed us by.
So we were lucky.
We made it to the border, and that was already wintertime.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Through the border, he had-- there were some smugglers who would smuggle people to the east.
But we had to go in snow, by foot, over the border.
When we reached the last station by train, we had to go over the border by foot.
And I remember I was in snow till my knees.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we made it.
We came over.
And that's where I found my brother.
He made his way by foot from the forest to Ostrog.
In Ostrog.
He was there with his family.
Yeah.
I went to his house, and my friend went there, too.
And-- but my friend, after a couple of days, when he looked at-- there were a lot of Russian soldiers around.
He was the elegant type.
When he looked at the Russian uniforms, he told me that he's not going to put on one of those.
So he made his way back to Przedborz.
No, he-- I heard that he was killed by-- I don't know.
He was involved in some monkey business or something.
I don't know.
I never saw him again.
I stayed with my brother.
My sister went to Lwow because--
Yeah.
It's-- you see, my brother and my sister married a brother and a sister from the other family.
It was a criss-cross match.
So my-- since they were very rich people in town, in Ostrog, when the Russians came in, the parents of-- the in-laws from the other side, they ran to Lwow because they were afraid of the Russians.
We were afraid of the Germans.
They were afraid of the Russians because they were the rich capitalists.
So whom I found in Ostrog is only my brother, his family.
My sister was already in Lwow.
Can you spell the name of that place for us?
Lwow?
L-W-O-W.
O with the--
--thing on top.
Oh, well, that was in '39 I arrived.
I stayed till, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till the Germans came in there, in '41.
But that was--
Yeah, that wasn't all.
I was there, which-- it was, you know, a lot of-- we got a lot of people from Poland to this town.
Because there are a lot of them run to the east to save--
I didn't hear anything.
No, I didn't hear anything.
No, at that time it was impossible already to go anyplace.
They didn't let you.
So I was there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And while-- this was-- Ostrog was a town where a lot of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very also, the-- most of the people there were speaking Russian.
That was the intelligentsia.
The Jewish intelligentsia were speaking Russian.
No.
But here in Ostrog, there was a guy, a man who-- I used to go there sometimes also for my vacations.
And he was an artist.
And he always used to make sketches of me.
He used to play the violin, artist and performer.
He was an artist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I arrived in '39, and during those two years, he was courting me.
And I eventually got married to him.
There, in Ostrog.
Yes.
OK.
